overview: Build a native Godot breakout game strongly inspired by DX-Ball 2 with bright candy-shop spectacle, multi-ball chaos, falling power-ups, juicy screen feedback, and an easy Windows export path for non-technical players.
openQuestions: Assume Windows desktop is the primary shipping target for the first release.
Assume Godot 4.x with GDScript is acceptable because the shipped deliverable is still a native executable package.
Assume procedural visuals and synth-free presentation are acceptable for version one if external art and music would slow delivery.

stories.title: Bootstrap a native Godot project
stories.description: Create the Godot project, main scene, repo hygiene, and Ralph task scaffolding so work is resumable inside the repo.
acceptanceCriteria: The repo contains a valid Godot project file and a main scene.
Ralph task state exists in .agents/tasks and progress notes exist in .ralph.
The project can be opened directly in Godot without additional manual setup.

stories.title: Implement the core arcade loop
stories.description: Add paddle, balls, collisions, bricks, power-up drops, lives, score, level flow, and at least one negative pickup.
acceptanceCriteria: The player can start, launch, clear bricks, lose balls, and progress to another board.
Multiple balls can be active at once without instantly destabilizing the game.
A bad pickup can punish the player in a clear way.

stories.title: Push the juice and ship it cleanly
stories.description: Add visual spectacle, persistence, packaging docs, and a Windows export script that produces a non-technical-player-friendly bundle.
acceptanceCriteria: The game has visible particles, glows, animated backgrounds, and strong title or game-over presentation.
There is a documented script that exports a Windows build into a dist folder.
